paper_title: Black Hole-Driven Identity Absorbing in Diffusion Models

core_story.problem: Introduction Diffusion models (DMs) have rapidly emerged as leading frameworks for high-quality generative tasks, excelling in applications like text-to-image synthesis, image editing, and inverse problem-solving [9, 26, 27, 32, 33, 40, 42].
core_story.limitation: However, the widespread use of DMs has raised significant privacy concerns.
core_story.insight: This motivates us to explore whether representations in the h-space can be modified to remove identity-related information, thereby preventing the generation of images associated with those identities.
core_story.proposed_method: To address these issues, we propose Black Hole-Driven Identity Absorption (BIA), a novel ap- proach for identity erasure within the latent space of dif- fusion models.
core_story.experimental_evidence: Both quantitative and qualitative results indicate that BIA effec- tively unlearns identities across multiple images of the same identity, achieving superior perceptual quality and attribute retention, outperforms baselines and GUIDE, a GAN-based unlearning approach.
core_story.summary: Introduction Diffusion models (DMs) have rapidly emerged as leading frameworks for high-quality generative tasks, excelling in applications like text-to-image synthesis, image editing, and inverse problem-solving [9, 26, 27, 32, 33, 40, 42]. However, the widespread use of DMs has raised significant privacy concerns. This motivates us to explore whether representations in the h-space can be modified to remove identity-related information, thereby preventing the generation of images associated with those identities. To address these issues, we propose Black Hole-Driven Identity Absorption (BIA), a novel ap- proach for identity erasure within the latent space of dif- fusion models. Both quantitative and qualitative results indicate that BIA effec- tively unlearns identities across multiple images of the same identity, achieving superior perceptual quality and attribute retention, outperforms baselines and GUIDE, a GAN-based unlearning approach.

1. Introduction
Diffusion models (DMs) have rapidly emerged as leading
frameworks for high-quality generative tasks, excelling in
applications like text-to-image synthesis, image editing, and
inverse problem-solving [9, 26, 27, 32, 33, 40, 42]. DMs
have set new standards for high-resolution image generation
by using conditional mechanisms, with impressive results
in blending diverse latent variables, particularly in text-to-

image applications [46, 49]. However, the widespread use
of DMs has raised significant privacy concerns. These mod-
els often draw from large, indiscriminately sourced datasets

introduction_structure.writing_strategy: Enumerates takeaways to make the paper's value explicit.
introduction_structure.evidence_text:
over, enforcing these standards remains challenging due to
the vast scope of training datasets. This highlights the need
for methods in DMs that enable selective content erasure,
concept removal, and identity unlearning, aligning partially
with privacy regulations and mitigating misuse [43, 47].
In response to these challenges, machine unlearning
(MU) has emerged as a strategy for selectively erasing spe-
cific information from trained models [5]. According to
[13], MU can be categorized into exact unlearning (which
requires full model retraining) and approximate unlearning
(which modifies the model to prevent the generation of spe-
cific concepts [39] or identities [17]). While exact unlearn-
ing is theoretically ideal, it is often impractical due to the
highly entangled nature of generative models.
Recent advancements in MU have demonstrated
progress in eliminating undesired content from diffusion
models, particularly in unlearning nudity [14], copyrighted
imagery, and specific artistic styles [17, 39]. However, re-
moving complex identities, such as those of well-known
public figures, remains particularly challenging due to their
deep integration within the model’s feature space

experiment_structure.purpose: Presents experimental evidence supporting the paper.
experiment_structure.author_introduction_strategy: Uses staged evidence from setup to comparison and analysis.

limitation_expression: However, as demand grows for content erasure and concept removal, privacy concerns highlight the need for identity disentan- glement in the latent space of diffusion models.
However, the widespread use of DMs has raised significant privacy concerns.
However, unlearning within generative models is not a straightforward analog to data deletion [53].
However, re- moving complex identities, such as those of well-known public figures, remains particularly challenging due to their deep integration within the model’s feature space [3].

However, diffusion models operate through complex, multi- step Markov chains, where each step holds a distinct la- tent representation [26, 44, 55], complicating interpretabil- ity compared to GANs’ unified latent space [20].
However, a star’s death is not the only path to black hole formation; various cosmic processes can create these gravitational giants [12].
insight_expression: This motivates us to explore whether representations in the h-space can be modified to remove identity-related information, thereby preventing the generation of images associated with those identities.
method_expression: To address these issues, we propose Black Hole-Driven Identity Absorption (BIA), a novel ap- proach for identity erasure within the latent space of dif- fusion models.

Our method effectively prevents the generation of a specified identity while preserving other attributes, as validated by improved scores on identity similarity (SID), FID metrics, qualitative evaluations, and user studies as compared to SOTA.
To address this, methods such as DiffAutoencoder [44] and Diffu- sionCLIP [32] introduce auxiliary encoders and fine-tuning to bring semantic control into DMs for effective editing.
Inspired by this phenomenon, we conceptualize the la- tent space in our framework as a “universe” and the identity we aim to remove as a “black hole”.

Our main contributions are as fol- lows: • To the best of our knowledge, this is the first explo- ration of identity unlearning within the h-space of diffu- sion models. • We propose the Black Hole-Driven Identity Absorption (BIA) framework, which selectively absorbs identifiable features within the latent space. • Our BIA pipeline is designed to wrap only the identity- specific features, allowing high-quality image generation without affecting other attributes. • Extensive experiments demonstrate that BIA achieves state-of-the-art performance in both qualitative and quan- titative evaluations, surpassing existing identity unlearn- ing methods [53].

raw_sections.abstract:
Abstract
Recent advances in diffusion models have positioned them
as powerful generative frameworks for high-resolution im-
age synthesis across diverse domains. The emerging “h-
space” within these models, defined by bottleneck activa-
tions in the denoiser, offers promising pathways for seman-
tic image editing similar to GAN latent spaces. However,
as demand grows for content erasure and concept removal,
privacy concerns highlight the need for identity disentan-
glement in the latent space of diffusion models. The high-
dimensional latent space poses challenges for identity re-
moval, as traversing with random or orthogonal directions
often leads to semantically unvalidated regions, resulting
in unrealistic outputs. To address these issues, we propose
Black Hole-Driven Identity Absorption (BIA), a novel ap-
proach for identity erasure within the latent space of dif-
fusion models. BIA uses a “black hole” metaphor, where
the latent region representing a specified identity acts as an
attractor, drawing in nearby latent points of surrounding
identities to “wrap” the black hole. Instead of relying on
random traversals for optimization, BIA employs an identity
absorption mechanism by attracting and wrapping nearby
validated latent points associated with other identities to
achieve a vanishing effect for specified identity. Our method
effectively prevents the generation of a specified identity
while preserving other attributes, as validated by improved
scores on identity similarity (SID), FID metrics, qualitative
evaluations, and user studies as compared to SOTA.
raw_sections.introduction:
1. Introduction
Diffusion models (DMs) have rapidly emerged as leading
frameworks for high-quality generative tasks, excelling in
applications like text-to-image synthesis, image editing, and
inverse problem-solving [9, 26, 27, 32, 33, 40, 42]. DMs
have set new standards for high-resolution image generation
by using conditional mechanisms, with impressive results
in blending diverse latent variables, particularly in text-to-
*Corresponding author
Figure 1. Visualization of our identity unlearning method on a
pre-trained diffusion model [42]. The second column shows the
latent point reconstruction of the original image, while the third
column displays the reconstruction after identity unlearning, pre-
serving other attributes and overall image quality.
image applications [46, 49]. However, the widespread use
of DMs has raised significant privacy concerns. These mod-
els often draw from large, indiscriminately sourced datasets
that may contain personal or sensitive information without
consent [30], prompting ethical issues around privacy, intel-
lectual property, and potential misuse [58].
Recent studies reveal that DMs can memorize and re-
produce specific training images, posing privacy risks [2, 8,
10]. The ability to recall sensitive data, such as facial im-
ages, has fueled concerns about misuse in areas like deep-
fake creation and unauthorized reproductions [7, 16, 58].
There is a growing need for methods that can ensure data
privacy, particularly regarding identity information embed-
ded within models [22, 35, 63]. Legal frameworks, includ-
ing the California Consumer Privacy Act (CCPA) [19] and
the EU’s GDPR [18], demand mechanisms like the “right
to be forgotten” [28]. In principle, this requires model to
behave as if the forgotten data had never been part of their
training. However, unlearning within generative models is
not a straightforward analog to data deletion [53]. More-
This CVPR paper is the Open Access version, provided by the Computer Vision Foundation.
Except for this watermark, it is identical to the accepted version;
the final published version of the proceedings is available on IEEE Xplore.
28544

over, enforcing these standards remains challenging due to
the vast scope of training datasets. This highlights the need
for methods in DMs that enable selective content erasure,
concept removal, and identity unlearning, aligning partially
with privacy regulations and mitigating misuse [43, 47].
In response to these challenges, machine unlearning
(MU) has emerged as a strategy for selectively erasing spe-
cific information from trained models [5]. According to
[13], MU can be categorized into exact unlearning (which
requires full model retraining) and approximate unlearning
(which modifies the model to prevent the generation of spe-
cific concepts [39] or identities [17]). While exact unlearn-
ing is theoretically ideal, it is often impractical due to the
highly entangled nature of generative models.
Recent advancements in MU have demonstrated
progress in eliminating undesired content from diffusion
models, particularly in unlearning nudity [14], copyrighted
imagery, and specific artistic styles [17, 39]. However, re-
moving complex identities, such as those of well-known
public figures, remains particularly challenging due to their
deep integration within the model’s feature space [3]. This
paper addresses the challenge of efficient identity unlearn-
ing within diffusion models—specifically, the ability to
modify the model’s latent space to suppress the generation
of certain identities, as defined by us and [53].
The latent space in generative models, particularly in
single-step models like GANs, encodes concepts that can
be linearly manipulated for attribute editing [41, 48, 54, 60].
However, diffusion models operate through complex, multi-
step Markov chains, where each step holds a distinct la-
tent representation [26, 44, 55], complicating interpretabil-
ity compared to GANs’ unified latent space [20]. To address
this, methods such as DiffAutoencoder [44] and Diffu-
sionCLIP [32] introduce auxiliary encoders and fine-tuning
to bring semantic control into DMs for effective editing.
DDMs already have a semantic latent space [34] also known
as h-space, At the bottleneck level of U-Net has just en-
tered the mainstream, allowing auxiliary editing networks
to carry out specific alterations. Several approaches [21, 68]
have been proposed to discover meaningful directions by
manipulating the vector in theh-space to alter the generated
image in a semantically meaningful way, such as “wearing a
hat”. This motivates us to explore whether representations
in the h-space can be modified to remove identity-related
information, thereby preventing the generation of images
associated with those identities.
In astrophysics, when a massive star collapses at the end
of its life, it forms a mysterious and powerful entity known
as a black hole [1, 36]. Only the most massive stars are des-
tined for this transformation, becoming stellar mass black
holes upon their demise. However, a star’s death is not the
only path to black hole formation; various cosmic processes
can create these gravitational giants [12]. Within a black
hole, gravity is so extreme that nothing can escape, com-
pressing matter into an incredibly dense point. This intense
gravitational pull draws in and merges surrounding matter,
erasing any trace of what existed. What lies inside a black
hole remains one of the biggest mysteries of the universe.
Inspired by this phenomenon, we conceptualize the la-
tent space in our framework as a “universe” and the identity
we aim to remove as a “black hole”. This leads us to pro-
pose a Black Hole-driven Identity Absorbing (BIA) frame-
work, where the specified identity behaves like a “dying
star”, forming into a black hole. In this designated region of
latent space, the black hole pulls in and merges nearby la-
tent identities, absorbing and unlearning the specified iden-
tity. In our BIA approach, only the identifiable features of
the specified identity are wrapped, leaving other attributes
in the latent space intact. Our main contributions are as fol-
lows:
• To the best of our knowledge, this is the first explo-
ration of identity unlearning within the h-space of diffu-
sion models.
• We propose the Black Hole-Driven Identity Absorption
(BIA) framework, which selectively absorbs identifiable
features within the latent space.
• Our BIA pipeline is designed to wrap only the identity-
specific features, allowing high-quality image generation
without affecting other attributes.
• Extensive experiments demonstrate that BIA achieves
state-of-the-art performance in both qualitative and quan-
titative evaluations, surpassing existing identity unlearn-
ing methods [53].
In summary, BIA provides a robust and effective approach
for identity unlearning in diffusion models.
raw_sections.method:
3. Proposed Approach
This section discusses our approach for identity unlearn-
ing through a black hole metaphor, as concept illustrated in
Fig. 2. We first discuss the formation of black hole region
in the latent space of DM as visualized in Fig. 3. In Fig. 4,
we present the main architecture of our proposed Black
Hole-Driven Identity Absorption (BIA) framework, which
illustrates the entire process of identifying and absorbing
identity-specific features within the latent space. For spe-
cific identity removal, we first map the input image to its
latent region using iDDPM [42]. Utilizing the semantic and
editable properties of h-space, we define the boundary of
this region by identifying adjacent latent codes, as proposed
in GUIDE [53], and by training a classifier to distinguish
between the target identity and surrounding identities, we
refer to this latent region as the “black hole”, symboliz-
ing the specified identity that will be removed. Once this
black hole region is formed, we locate the k nearest latent
points to wrap that region. By optimizing identity features
between the black hole and these k points, we effectively
wrap the black hole, removing the specified identity while
preserving other attributes.
raw_sections.experiments:
4. Experiments
In this section, we assess the effectiveness of our BIA
method through comparative, user, and an ablation studies
on the CelebA-HQ dataset [31]. All experiments were per-
formed at a resolution of 256 × 256 for high-quality visu-
alization. We operate within the h-space, following [34]
for its semantic advantages like homogeneity, linearity, and
robustness. To define the black hole region, we use a lin-
ear SVM classifier from the sklearn Python package,
with parameters matching the latent space dimensionality
(dh = 8×8×512 = 32, 768) based on the U-Net in DDPM
[50]. Approximately, 1000+ images were used to find the
good decision boundary for the identity direction in each ex-
periment. In the unlearning phase, we apply 1,500 iterations
with 1,000 time steps to effectively remove identity-specific
features, with minimal computational cost. For inference,
we adopt a step-skipping strategy, allowing us to generate
high-quality images within 40-100 denoising steps, reduc-
ing inference time to 5-10 seconds on a single RTX-4090.
raw_sections.conclusion:
6. Conclusion
In this paper, we present the Black Hole-Driven Identity
Absorption (BIA) framework, a pioneering approach for
identity unlearning in the h-space of diffusion models. By
leveraging the concept of “black hole”, BIA removes spe-
cific identity features while preserving other attributes, en-
suring high-quality and realistic image generation. Both
quantitative and qualitative results indicate that BIA effec-
tively unlearns identities across multiple images of the same
identity, achieving superior perceptual quality and attribute
retention, outperforms baselines and GUIDE, a GAN-based
unlearning approach. This framework offers a promising
solution for privacy focused, ethical AI applications, bal-
ancing identity removal with generative performance.
Acknowledgment This work was supported by the BK21
FOUR project (AI-driven Convergence Software Education
Research Program) funded by the Ministry of Education,
Kyungpook National University, Korea (4120240214871),
and the ICT R&D program of MSIT/IITP (RS-2024-
00336663, Development of AI technology for tracking and
investigating drug criminals through multimedia sources).
